metric_value: ASEP was a two-way power-line communications system controlled by a Data General Eclipse S-230 minicomputer with 256K core memory
Data General Eclipse S-230 with 256K core memory served as ASEP system controller
Florida Power Corp piloted dynamic rate system charging customers peak rates only when peak levels actually reached, polling meters as often as every 30 minutes
Wisconsin Electric used ASEP to control water heaters in 154,000 homes plus remotely meter customers
Florida Power & Light planned ASEP for residential AC/heating system and electrical-water-heater control
DoE estimated most US states would use time-of-day rates within five or six years
notes: Directionally correct over 30+ years; speed varied by jurisdiction.

metric_value: Abbott (AS&E VP): 'If electrical usage reaches dangerous levels, the system is capable of turning off blocks of non-essential electrical loads' to avert major problems
Annis (AS&E president): time-of-day rating supports the national goal of energy conservation
Utilities in Missouri, New Jersey, Minnesota, and California also implemented ASEP; ~12 states using such systems experimentally
Time-of-day rates intended to enable consumers to save by shifting washing-machine, dryer, and water-heater use to evenings or weekends
Two-way ASEP system prevents electrical brownouts and blackouts by operating over existing power lines, an already-in-place communications medium
